Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I had a chunk of free time and then try to cram in what I could, but it never felt organized. Being 26 and juggling other responsibilities, I'd often study late at night when things quieted down. I preferred listening to recorded lectures while doing other things around the house, which worked better for me than reading. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was going on in my life. Deadlines definitely pushed me to study more intensely, but otherwise I'd drift through the weeks without much structure. I don't have reliable internet at home, so I had to plan around when I could get to places with wifi. That made regular scheduling nearly impossible, and I just adapted to whatever worked in the moment.
Q3: Usually it was because an assignment was due or I needed to check something specific. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if I'd missed anything important since I couldn't always keep track. The discussion posts were something I'd check when I actually made it online, since I found hearing other people's perspectives kind of interesting. But honestly, a lot of times I logged in out of obligation more than genuine curiosity.
Q4: Not really a routine, more like bursts of activity when I managed to get online. There were a few weeks where I got into a rhythm of checking in at the library every couple of days. But that didn't last because life kept getting in the way. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my day—it was always something I had to make a specific effort to access.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I'd watch them and sometimes replay sections when I didn't catch something the first time. The discussion forums were also helpful because reading other students' takes on topics gave me different angles I hadn't considered. Anything I could absorb through audio stuck with me better than dense reading materials.
Q6: I mostly ignored the supplementary readings and extra resources. With limited internet access and low resources, I couldn't afford to spend time on things that felt optional. The textbook links and external articles just piled up in my to-do list and never got touched. I figured if it wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it probably wasn't worth the hassle of accessing it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course. If it was going to help me on an assignment or the exam, I'd prioritize it. I also valued things that didn't require me to be constantly online—anything I could download or listen to once worked well for me. Honestly, at this point in my life, I need things to be practical and efficient.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, time-wise. Required ones had that pressure attached, so I'd make sure to do them even if I wasn't feeling it. With optional stuff, I'd think about doing it and then something else would come up and I'd forget. There was no real consequence for skipping them, so they always fell to the bottom of my priorities.
Q9: Absolutely. Once I downloaded lecture recordings or materials, I'd study them offline constantly. I'd listen to audio files on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also borrowed textbooks from friends and took handwritten notes that I'd review later. A lot of my actual learning happened completely disconnected from the internet.
Q10: Most of my studying happened offline, so the LMS wouldn't capture any of that. I'd spend hours going over my notes or listening to downloaded lectures, but none of that would show up anywhere. Sometimes I'd have study sessions with classmates where we'd talk through concepts, and that was really valuable but invisible. The LMS only saw me when I was actively logged in, which was a fraction of my study time.
Q11: All the time. I'd download videos or PDFs during my library visits and then work with them for days or weeks offline. One login might mean hours of studying later that the system never knew about. It was just more practical for me given my internet situation. I treated the LMS like a pickup point rather than a place to actually study.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just felt completely bored and disconnected from the material. The course content didn't always feel relevant to my life, and at 26, I sometimes questioned why I was even doing this. When my anxiety spiked, I'd avoid logging in because facing the workload felt overwhelming. Those periods usually coincided with other life stress piling up.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with personal stuff, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Not having reliable internet made everything harder—I couldn't just quickly check something when I had a question. Competing responsibilities always won out because they felt more immediate and real. The course often felt like this abstract thing I'd get to eventually.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd watch a video but my mind would be elsewhere, thinking about bills or other worries. Sometimes I'd open readings and just stare at them, not really processing the words. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt tedious or confusing. If an assignment seemed complicated, I'd tell myself I'd figure it out later and then scramble at the last minute. The CBT sessions I had helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was another story. I knew it was self-sabotage, but the immediate relief of avoidance always felt better in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd coast along ignoring the LMS until a deadline loomed, then suddenly I'd be logging in frantically. It wasn't healthy, but it was effective enough to get me through. The pressure of a deadline cut through my boredom and forced me to focus, even if just temporarily.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When it was positive, I'd feel a brief surge of motivation that faded quickly. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage even more because it confirmed my doubts about whether I belonged here. I appreciated detailed feedback when I got it, but it didn't fundamentally change my patterns.
Q18: The structure felt pretty rigid, which didn't work great for someone in my situation. Having set deadlines helped in some ways, but the expectation of regular online participation was tough without consistent internet. I would've benefited from more flexibility in how and when I could engage. The course assumed everyone had equal access, which just wasn't true for me.
Q19: The heavy reliance on online resources made things harder for me. If there had been more downloadable content or offline-friendly options, I would've engaged more consistently. The discussion requirements were actually okay because I could batch my participation when I had access. But anything requiring real-time engagement was basically impossible for my situation.
Q20: I think people might be exploring, trying to figure out what's important, or just anxious about missing something. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes you're just clicking around feeling lost. For me, there were times I'd browse a lot but not feel confident enough to actually submit work. It's like window shopping versus actually buying something.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. A lot of my learning happened offline where no one could track it. Someone could look at my low attendance and login numbers and assume I wasn't trying, but I was putting in hours with downloaded materials. The LMS doesn't capture studying that happens away from the screen. Performance isn't always tied to visible online activity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you logged in, what you clicked on, how long you stayed. But they can't show understanding or effort that happens offline. They miss the context of why someone might be absent or inactive. They also can't capture the quality of engagement, just the quantity. A lot of real learning is invisible to any tracking system.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's wrestling with ideas and connecting them to your life. For me, engagement happens in fits and starts, often when something finally clicks or feels relevant. It's internal more than external.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my offline study sessions, my conversations with classmates, or the mental work I did processing lectures while doing other things. It wouldn't capture my struggles with internet access or how that shaped my participation patterns. It also wouldn't show that despite my boredom and low mood after the intervention, I still cared enough to pass. The data tells a very incomplete story.
